summary: Ronacher reflects on his roots in the Python community and how AI-driven "vibe coding" is bringing a new wave of people into programming. He argues that if you build software—whether by hand or with an agent—you are a programmer, and the community must embrace that reality. While he has seen dismissive attitudes toward AI, he observes a shift toward acceptance and expects AI to increase, not reduce, the number of programmers. The real risk is not code quality but the loss of human mentorship and community connection when AI is the entry point. He concludes that Python should actively welcome, mentor, and integrate these newcomers so they grow beyond tool dependence.
key_points: AI tools are creating programmers overnight, and the community should recognize them as legitimate.
AI is likely to expand the programming population, not shrink it, making this a major inclusion moment for Python.
The main danger is isolation: AI introductions bypass human mentors and community on-ramps.
Python should proactively reach out to vibe coders to teach engineering lessons and avoid vendor lock-in.
money_quotes: If you're painting (even your very first painting) you are a painter.
Consequently if you create a program, by hand or with the aid of an agent, you are a programmer.
AI will bring more people into programming than anything else we have done in the last decade.
We need to pay close attention to vibe coding.
It would be a mistake to treat them as outcasts or “not real” programmers.
That lack of human connection is, I think, the biggest downside.
We need to turn a solitary interaction with an AI into a shared journey with a community.
key_insight: AI-driven coding will grow the programmer population, so communities must actively welcome and mentor these newcomers to replace missing human connections and prevent tool lock-in.
